coredata.dc:title: Portable and intelligent ratio fluorometry and colorimetry for dual-mode detection of dopamine based on B, N-codoped carbon dots and machine learning
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaoshuang
Wang, Lijun
Wu, Mengnan
Zheng, Yihao
Wang, Ruirui
Shao, Tong
Zeng, Suyuan
Li, Aifeng
Li, Rui
Yue, Qiaoli
coredata.dc:description: A dual-mode approach was developed for dopamine (DA) assay based on boron (B) and nitrogen (N) co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs). This platform enabled highly sensitive and specific detection of DA in biological samples through collaborative ratio fluorometry and colorimetry. B, N-CDs were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method using 3-aminobenzylboric acid (3-APBA) and proline (Pro) as precursors. B, N-CDs exhibited dark blue fluorescence under ultraviolet (UV) light excitation with a quantum yield (QY) of 0.05. In the presence of DA, the fluorescence of B, N-CDs displayed bright blue with the QY at 0.22. The original fluorescence peak intensity at 420 nm of B, N-CDs decreased, while a new peak at 465 nm increased significantly with the increasing concentration of DA. In this case, a ratio fluorometry for DA detection was constructed using B, N-CDs as fluorescence probes. On the other way, the color of B, N-CDs solution changed from colorless to brown with the addition of DA. A colorimetry for DA sensing was established based on the absorbance enhancement of B, N-CDs. There is excellent linear relationship within the concentration range of 2.5–500 μM for DA sensing with limit of detection (LOD) at 0.22 μM (ratio fluorometry) and 1.04 μM (colorimetry) and the relative standard deviations (RSD%) are 0.2170 and 0.02131, respectively. To enable the real-time visual and portable quantification of DA, two intelligent methods were explored by a program named RGB color analysis in a smartphone and machine learning. This dual-mode sensing strategy combined high sensitivity, wide linear range and ease of operation, offering a novel solution for rapid analysis of DA in complex biological substrates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine
Carbon dots
Dual-mode detection
Machine learning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of portable cotton swabs based on B, N-CDs
Intelligent detection of DA using machine learning

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of (A) B, N-CDs (inset: the corresponding size distribution) and (B) single B, N-CDs particle, (C) HRTEM image of B, N-CDs + DA and (D) XRD pattern of B, N-CDs.
(A) FT-IR of B, N-CDs and B, N-CDs + DA, (B) XPS full spectrum of B, N-CDs and HR-XPS of (C) C1s, (D) N1s, (E) O1s, (F) B1s.

(A) Effect of common ions and small molecules on the fluorescence spectrum of B, N-CDs (inset: photos of B, N-CDs + ions/molecules in UV light), (B) anti-interference performance of B, N-CDs for fluorescent detection of DA, (C) effect of common ions and small molecules on the absorbance of B, N-CDs (inset: photos of B, N-CDs + ions/molecules in natural light), (D) anti-interference performance of B, N-CDs for colorimetric detection of DA (Conditions: B, N-CDs, 0.0358 mg mL−1; pH, 9; DA, 150 μM; other species, 300 μM).

(A) Photos of B, N-CDs solution with DA in (Ⅰ) UV light and (Ⅲ) day light, (Ⅲ) portable swabs under (Ⅱ) UV lamp and (Ⅳ) daylight lamp, (B) fluorescence spectra of B, N-CDs in the presence of DA with different concentrations, (C) linear relationship between F465/F420 and DA concentration, (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of B, N-CDs in the presence of DA with different concentrations, (E) linear relationship between absorbance and DA concentration, (F) linear relationship between G/B and DA concentration (Conditions: B, N-CDs, 0.0358 mg mL−1; pH, 9).

Prediction of B, N-CDs + DA concentration in actual solutions using different datasets and machine learning models: (A) R2 values; (B) MAE values; (C) data distribution plot of predicted vs actual values for D_8-RFR; (D) feature importance analysis for D_8-RFR; (E) Kernel density estimate plot for six features of D_8-RFR.
Synthesis and application of B, N-CDs in detection of DA.
Results of DA detection using fluorometry and/or colorimetry.
Results of DA detection in real samples of plasma and urine.

head.ce:title: Portable and intelligent ratio fluorometry and colorimetry for dual-mode detection of dopamine based on B, N-codoped carbon dots and machine learning

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, 252059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, 252059, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-mode approach was developed for dopamine (DA) assay based on boron (B) and nitrogen (N) co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs). This platform enabled highly sensitive and specific detection of DA in biological samples through collaborative ratio fluorometry and colorimetry. B, N-CDs were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method using 3-aminobenzylboric acid (3-APBA) and proline (Pro) as precursors. B, N-CDs exhibited dark blue fluorescence under ultraviolet (UV) light excitation with a quantum yield (QY) of 0.05. In the presence of DA, the fluorescence of B, N-CDs displayed bright blue with the QY at 0.22. The original fluorescence peak intensity at 420 nm of B, N-CDs decreased, while a new peak at 465 nm increased significantly with the increasing concentration of DA. In this case, a ratio fluorometry for DA detection was constructed using B, N-CDs as fluorescence probes. On the other way, the color of B, N-CDs solution changed from colorless to brown with the addition of DA. A colorimetry for DA sensing was established based on the absorbance enhancement of B, N-CDs. There is excellent linear relationship within the concentration range of 2.5–500 μM for DA sensing with limit of detection (LOD) at 0.22 μM (ratio fluorometry) and 1.04 μM (colorimetry) and the relative standard deviations (RSD%) are 0.2170 and 0.02131, respectively. To enable the real-time visual and portable quantification of DA, two intelligent methods were explored by a program named RGB color analysis in a smartphone and machine learning. This dual-mode sensing strategy combined high sensitivity, wide linear range and ease of operation, offering a novel solution for rapid analysis of DA in complex biological substrates.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), as a neurotransmitter in brain, regulates motor control, behavioral selection, and reinforcement learning in the central nervous system [1,2]. Disruptions in its system are linked to conditions such as Parkinson's, schizophrenia, Tourette's syndrome, attention deficit hyperactive disorder (ADHD), and pituitary tumors [3]. Excess DA may lead to hypertension, strokes, irritability, emotional dysregulation, and nervous system damage, as seen in Huntington's disease [4,5]. Therefore, it is necessary and important to find an approach to determine DA in biological samples. For this purpose, there are various methods for the detection of DA in vitro and/or in vivo such as chromatography [6], electrochemistry [7–9], electrochemiluminescence [10], chemiluminescence [11], colorimetry [12] as well as fluorometry [13]. Among these methods, fluorescence method exhibits wide application in monitoring DA due to its excellent sensitivity and specificity, rapid response and easy to realize real-time analysis. These fluorescent systems were usually employed to monitor DA based on small organic molecules [14,15] and especially nanomaterials including metal nanoclusters [16], metal-organic frameworks [17], as well as carbon dots (CDs) [18].

CDs, as carbon nanoparticles less than 10 nm in size with intense fluorescence, were widely applied in optical devices, photocatalysis, drug loading, sensing and bioimaging [13]. By comparison with traditional organic dyes and quantum dots, CDs display excellent photostability, high quantum yield, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [19]. For the analysis of DA, there are possible interactions between CDs and DA including electron transfer, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions and π-π stacking. Multiple functional groups on the surface of CDs can interact with amino groups of DA [13]. It was reported that CDs were prepared from ferrocene and used for DA sensing in the range of 3.3–500 μM with the limit of detection (LOD) at 5.54 μM [20]. In a similar work, CDs synthesized from Epimedium showed yellow fluorescence [21]. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched by DA in the concentration range of 0.1–1000 μM (LOD, 0.087 μM) due to the electrostatic interaction between CDs and DA. These CDs employed as fluorescent probes for monitoring DA were basically based on fluorescence quenching mode. By comparison with fluorescence quenching systems, ratio fluorimetry shows high sensitivity and accuracy, and excellent specificity and repeatability especially for the phenomenon of peak wavelength shift.

Due to the complex derivatization process of DA, traditional detection methods typically require the assistance of large-scale instrumentation, making in situ analysis challenging. Therefore, it is essential to develop a portable approach for DA determination. In portable sensing analysis, filter paper [22] and cotton swabs [23] are commonly employed as sample carriers and collection materials due to their cost-effectiveness, ease of disposal, and operational simplicity. Cotton swabs, in particular serve as effective sampling tools, offering advantages such as non-invasiveness, ease of handling, and accessibility, especially for collecting surface residues and body fluid samples. These cotton swabs have been widely used in colorimetric [23] and Raman-based methods [24], while their application in fluorescence analysis remains limited. Currently, the market demand for DA detection has shifted from reliance on large-scale instruments to the development of highly sensitive and portable devices capable of on-site analysis. Visual detection methods remain among the most intuitive approaches for this purpose. In particularly, smartphone has emerged as promising analytical tools owing to their portability, user-friendliness, and efficient data processing capability. These devices can capture color images and convert them into quantifiable red, green, and blue (RGB) values, enabling the establishment of regression models that correlate RGB values with analyte concentration [25]. Machine learning algorithm, renowned for its ability to process large and complex datasets, can uncover valuable insights and patterns. It can enhance prediction accuracy and serve as a powerful tool for quantitative analysis across diverse fields. The integration of machine learning to automate the construction of quantitative model can improve significantly the speed and reliability of measurement [26].

In this study, dual-mode method based on ratio fluorometry and colorimetry was employed to detect DA in biological samples using CDs as probes with high sensitivity and specificity. Boron (B) and nitrogen (N) codoped-CDs (B, N-CDs) were prepared by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. 3-aminobenzeneboronic acid (3-APBA) and proline (Pro) were used as precursor materials for B, C, and N sources. On one hand, B, N-CDs dispersion showed dark blue fluorescence in ultraviolet (UV) light while it appeared bright blue in the presence of DA. The fluorescence intensity at 420 nm decreased slowly and the new peak of 465 nm increased significantly with increasing concentration of DA. In this case, the ratio fluorometry of DA was constructed. On the other hand, the color of B, N-CDs dispersion turned to brown with the addition of DA. The colorimetry of DA was built based on the color change of B, N-CDs. Moreover, cotton swabs for fluorometry and smartphone and machine learning for colorimetry of DA were developed to enhance the diversity and completeness of the method. The color change of B, N-CDs was combined with smartphone and machine learning for visible and intelligent detection of DA in biological samples. The preparation and application of B, N-CDs were illustrated in Scheme 1 .

B, N-CDs products were prepared in a DZF-6050 vacuum drying oven (Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA). The fluorescence spectra were recorded using an F-7000 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of B, N-CDs were recorded using a LUH4150 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Electron microscopy characterization of the samples was conducted using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected by a D/Max-2500 diffractometer (Rigaku Co, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Ltd, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded using a K-Alpha spectrometer. Ultimate 3000 high performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC, Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA) was used as a control method for the detection of real samples.

3-APBA, DA, NaCl, CaCl2, KCl, NaOH, NaCO3, NaHCO3 and quinine sulfate (QS) were procured from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH) and adenosine monophosphate (AMP) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Lysine (Lys), histidine (His), cysteine (Cys), urea, glucose (Glu) and Pro were ordered from Shanghai Perfemiker Material Technology Co (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiment.

B, N-CDs were synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method (Scheme 1). Briefly, 13.7 mg 3-APBA and 57.6 mg Pro were mixed in 20 mL DI water and ultrasonicated for 5 min, which were transferred to a 25 mL hydrothermal autoclave reactor. The mixture was heated at 180 °C for 6 h in an oven and cooled to room temperature, and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and dialyzed (1000 Da) for 24 h. The purified B, N-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of portable cotton swabs based on B, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The portable swab comprised a hollow cylindrical plastic tube with a sealed terminal end and an open induction zone at the opposite terminus, both extremities being enveloped with absorbent cotton matrices. For device activation, 120 μL of B, N-CDs solution was precisely injected into the plastic tube through the induction zone. The terminal regions were then meticulously wrapped with absorbent cotton to form a dual-headed swab configuration, with the sealed end distinctly marked with a blue annular indicator for identification purposes. The prepared portable sensor was aliquoted and stored at 4 °C for subsequent experimental applications.

Detection of DA was performed using fluorescence and colorimetric analysis. A series of DA standard solutions was prepared, and 2 μL aliquots (37.5 mM) were mixed with 250 μL of B, N-CDs solution (0.0358 mg mL−1). Fluorescence (350–550 nm, λex = 320 nm) and UV–vis absorption spectra (350–650 nm) were recorded, with parallel experiments conducted with and without DA for comparison.

For portable sensing, the sensing area of swabs was vertically aligned. Upon breaking the sealed end, one end of the plastic tube was open, allowing the B, N-CDs solution to migrate toward the sensing zone under gravity and pressure. Here, the solution interacted with DA in the cotton matrix, enabling dual-mode detection: (i) fluorescence changes under 365 nm UV light and (ii) visible color changes under ambient light.

The quantum yield (QY) of B, N-CDs was determined using a reference method with QS as the standard (0.1 M, Φst = 54 %, ηst = 1.33),with the detailed process in Section S1 (in Supplementary data) [23].

Biological samples including murine plasma and urine, as well as human urine, were collected for pretreatment according to references [12]. All samples were obtained using sterile containers and subsequently processed by centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 20 min. The resulting supernatant samples were carefully collected and stored at 4 °C for subsequent analysis.

To build a robust machine learning model, four different machine learning methods were employed to predict the concentration of B, N-CDs + DA standard solutions, including Random Forest Regression (RFR) [27], Support Vector Regression (SVR) [28], Ridge Regression (Ridge) [29], and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) [30]. The original training set was randomly split into a new training set and a validation set in a 4:1 ratio. The training set was used for model training, while the validation set was used to assess the model's accuracy in predicting the concentration of B, N-CDs + DA standard solutions. Finally, the test set was used to evaluate the model's performance in predicting DA content in real samples. The evaluation methods included calculating the coefficient of determination (R2) and the Mean Absolute Error (MAE).

To provide more feature information to the machine learning model, the KMeans [31] algorithm was utilized for feature expansion. For the training set of the machine learning model, 140 samples were obtained from image recognition, each containing the target concentration (B, N-CDs + DA standard solution) and the three features R, G, and B. The KMeans algorithm was applied to generate eight sets of clustering centroids based on the R, G, and B features, with the optimal values automatically iterated to the algorithm. The first iteration generated one centroid and the second iteration generated two centroids, and so on, with the final iteration generating eight centroids. Finally, the Euclidean distance between the clustering centroids and the sample points was calculated to obtain the expanded features. These expanded features were sequentially named K_1, K_2, …, K_8. Detailed test for specific machine learning model was provided in Section S2 (in Supplementary data).

Before characterization, four experimental conditions were optimized including the molar ratio of Pro to 3-APBA, pH, concentration of B, N-CDs and ionic strength in Section S3 (in Supplementary data), with the detailed results shown in Fig. S2 (in Supplementary data), Fig. S3 (in Supplementary data), Fig. S4 (in Supplementary data) and Fig. S5 (in Supplementary data). Following the optimization of conditions, the molar ratio of Pro to 3-APBA at 5:1 combined with pH of 9.0, the concentration of B, N-CDs at 0.0358 mg mL−1 were established as the optimal parameters for subsequent investigations.

The optical properties of B, N-CDs were investigated, as illustrated in Fig. S6 (in Supplementary data). UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S6A, in Supplementary data) exhibits a prominent absorption band centered at 280 nm, which can be attributed to n-π∗ transition of C–O bond [32]. To obtain the optimal excitation wavelength, a series of fluorescence spectra are recorded at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. S6B, in Supplementary data). Analysis of the fluorescence intensity reveals a significant increase of peak intensity as the excitation wavelength increases from 300 nm to 320 nm, following a gradual decrease when the excitation wavelength extended 340 nm. Based on these observations, 320 nm is identified as the optimal excitation wavelength for achieving maximum fluorescence intensity. The QY of B, N-CDs calculated is 5.0 %. Notably, upon addition of DA, the QY of the B, N-CDs + DA system significantly increases to 22.0 %, demonstrating a 4.4-fold enhancement in quantum efficiency (Fig. S7, in Supplementary data).

HRTEM characterization reveals that B, N-CDs exhibit spherical morphology with excellent dispersibility in aqueous solution, without observable aggregation (Fig. 1 A). Particle size distribution (Fig. 1A inset) analysis indicates that the diameter of B, N-CDs ranges from 1.6 nm to 3.6 nm. It is obvious that the size of B, N-CDs is uniform, with an average particle size of 2.6 ± 0.2 nm. Moreover, the crystalline property of B, N-CDs is shown in Fig. 1B. The inset shows the atomic-scale structure of an individual B, N-CDs nanoparticle, revealing distinct lattice fringe with a calculated interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm. HRTEM images of B, N-CDs + DA illustrate the obvious aggregation of B, N-CDs (Fig. 1C).

As depicted in Fig. 1D, the XRD pattern of the B, N-CDs exhibits characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 14.2°, 18.1°, 19.5°, 20.8°, 23.2°, 24.2°, and 26.6°, which correspond to the (012), (101), (002), (110), (215), (101), and (124) crystal planes, respectively. The dominant peak at 19.5° is ascribed to the (002) crystal plane of graphite. However, its interplanar spacing is notably smaller than that of pristine graphite (0.34 nm), indicating a lattice compression effect induced by B element (B substitution for C atom). This observation aligns with the lattice distortion characteristics revealed by HRTEM analysis. In the low-angle region (14.2°–18.1°), the (012) and (101) crystal planes likely correspond to the characteristic peaks of monoclinic B byproducts, which are hypothesized to arise from the partial decomposition of the precursor, 3-APBA, during high-temperature reactions. Notably, weak peaks observed in the range of 20°–30° exhibit broadening, attributed to nanoscale effects of CDs. According to Scherrer's equation, the diffraction peaks of small-size grains broaden or even vanish due to the limited coherent scattering domain, manifesting as short-range ordered fringe-like contrast. These findings suggest local lattice distortions, high-density defects (such as carbon vacancies or edge defects), and amorphous-nanocrystalline composite structures within B, N-CDs.

Further investigation into the surface characteristics is conducted using FT-IR and XPS. FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 A) reveal the presence of abundant –NH2 and –OH groups on the B, N-CDs surface, as evidenced by broad absorption bands in the 3000-3600 cm−1 region, corresponding to N–H and O–H stretching vibrations [33]. These hydrophilic functional groups significantly enhance the water solubility of B, N-CDs. Characteristic absorption peaks at 1575 cm−1 and 1435 cm−1 are attributed to C–C and C–N stretching vibrations [33]. Additional vibrational modes are identified at 1192 cm−1 (B–O/C–N), 961 cm−1 (C–N–C/C–O–C), and 880 cm−1 (B–OH and B–C/C–OH). The spectral region of 950-650 cm−1 is characteristic of C–H bending vibration in aromatic ring [34]. Collectively, these spectroscopic features confirm the successful incorporation of various functional groups and effective doping of B and N elements into the CDs via one-pot synthesis.

XPS analysis provides detailed insights into the elemental composition and chemical bonding states of B, N-CDs. The survey spectrum (Fig. 2B) displays distinct peaks at binding energies of 191.1 eV (B1s), 284.8 eV (C1s), 400.0 eV (N1s), and 532.3 eV (O1s). Deconvolution of C1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) yields four peaks at 283.4 eV (C–B), 284.6 eV (C–C), 285.8 eV (C–O/C–N), and 288.0 eV (CO) [35]. N1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) exhibits two characteristic peaks at 398.9 eV (pyridine N) and 400.8 eV (pyrrole N) [36]. O1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) reveals three components at 530.9 eV (CO), 532.2 eV (B–O), and 535.4 eV (C–OH/O–C–O) [37]. B1s spectrum (Fig. 2F) shows two distinct peaks at 191.3 eV (B–C) and 191.5 eV (B–O) [38]. Quantitative elemental analysis (Fig. 2B inset) determines the atomic composition to be 69.2 %, 23.6 %, 6.5 %, and 0.7 % for C, O, N and B, respectively. XPS results are in excellent agreement with FT-IR data, confirming the presence of various functional groups including CO, C–N, C–B, COOH, and OH on the B, N-CDs surface.

Ratio fluorometry Systematic investigation was conducted into the specificity of B, N-CDs for DA sensing under alkaline conditions (Fig. 3 A). To evaluate the selectivity of the system, the fluorescence response of B, N-CDs was examined in the presence of 12 common biomolecules including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cys, His, Lys, Urea, AA, Glu, GSH, ATP, and AMP. As shown in Fig. 3A, it is demonstrated that DA uniquely induces a significant enhancement in fluorescence intensity accompanied by a distinct blue shift in the emission spectrum of B, N-CDs. From the corresponding photos it can also be observed that only DA can brighten the fluorescence of B, N-CDs in UV light (Fig. 3A inset). The fluorescence enhancement of B, N-CDs toward DA was remarkably retained even under conditions where the concentration of interfering substances exceeded twice of DA, as demonstrated in Fig. 3B.

Colorimetry Regarding colorimetric detection of DA, Fig. 3C reveals that DA exclusively induces significant absorption peak in B, N-CDs solutions, leading to a substantial increase in absorbance of B, N-CDs, while other substances show negligible change of absorbance. The digital photos of B, N-CDs solutions appear brown in natural light only in the presence of DA (Fig. 3C inset). The specificity of DA detection is further emphasized by anti-interfering test in which the concentration of DA is half of those potential interfering substances (Fig. 3D). These results demonstrate the remarkable specificity and sensitivity of B, N-CDs for DA detection in complex biological substrates. For the anti-interfering experiment, the same result is shown in Fig. S8 (in Supplementary data). Therefore, B, N-CDs are suitable as colorimetric probes for the highly specific detection of DA.

Ratio fluorometry B, N-CDs solution displays weak fluorescence when excited at 365 nm, while it demonstrate significant emission enhancement with increasing concentration of DA (Fig. 4 A Ⅰ). In the presence of DA (0, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400, and 500 μM), the emission peaks of B, N-CDs exhibit a pronounced blue shift, accompanied by a marked enhancement in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4B). Fig. 4C depicts clearly the fluorescence enhancement of B, N-CDs in the presence of DA. Meanwhile, there exists a good linear response between the ratio intensity and DA concentration with an equation of F465/F420 = 0.2319 [DA] - 1.195 (R2 = 0.9936) in the range of 2.5–500 μM.

Colorimetry B, N-CDs solution appears colorless and transparent in natural light, while it turns brown gradually with the increasing concentration of DA (Fig. 4A Ⅲ). The maximum absorbance also shows a significant increase (Fig. 4D). Furthermore, there is a good linearity between absorbance and DA concentration (A = 0.003150 [DA] +0.1105 (R2 = 0.9903)) ranging from 2.5 μM to 500 μM in Fig. 4E. LOD values are determined to be 0.22 μM and 1.04 μM for the ratio fluorometry and colorimetry of DA, using the IUPAC-recommended equation of 3.29 S
B
/m (where S
B represents the standard deviation of the blank sample, n = 3; m denotes the slope of the calibration curve).

Portable test For the portable detection of DA, B, N-CDs-modified cotton swabs are employed to detect DA in real samples. In UV light, the sensing region of the cotton swab exhibits progressively bright blue fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 4A Ⅱ. Under daylight lamp, the sensing cotton turns to brown (Fig. 4A Ⅳ). By analyzing the colorimetric changes of the B, N-CDs + DA system under natural light, RGB values are obtained using image processing software, and the ratio of G/B is calculated based on the varying B values. A good linearity shows in Fig. 4F with a linear equation of G/B = 0.006150 [DA] +1.132 (R2 = 0.9970) in the range of 2.5–500 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intelligent detection of DA using machine learning
ce:section.ce:para: Following feature selection on the nine datasets, machine learning models are built according to the previously mentioned methods, with R2 and MAE values obtained. In Table S1 (in Supplementary data), the number of remaining features after feature selection is listed for different datasets, along with R2 and MAE values calculated for each dataset across machine learning. Under identical conditions, strong performance is observed in all models for both training and validation sets, indicating that accurate predictions of B, N-CDs with DA standard solution concentrations can be achieved by the developed machine learning models. However, significant performance discrepancies are identified in the test set.

The R2 and MAE values for predicting B, N-CDs + DA concentrations in actual solutions are shown in Fig. 5 A and B, using different datasets and models. When only RGB features are utilized, low R2 values and high MAE values are observed across all four machine learning methods, suggesting poor predictive capability for real samples. After feature expansion via the KMeans algorithm, improved R2 values and reduced MAE values are achieved in all methods. Optimal performance is obtained with the D_8 dataset combined with the RFR method (D_8-RFR), exhibiting R2 = 0.9642 and MAE = 0.5306, indicating robust prediction of DA concentration in real samples. Conversely, the poorest prediction performance is observed with the D_2-RFR model, despite excellent training set performance, leading to the suspicion of overfitting.

The predicted actual value distribution for the D_8-RFR model is displayed in Fig. 5C, showing data points clustered around the central line without significant deviations. Furthermore, feature importance analysis results for D_8-RFR are presented in Fig. 5D, highlighting that KMeans-expanded features are assigned significantly higher importance than original R and G features. Among these, the K_4 feature is identified as the most critical, with an importance value of 0.4129. To further investigate these features, Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) plots of six features are generated (Fig. 5E), revealing the distribution patterns at different concentration levels. Compared to original R and G features, sharper peak separations at distinct concentrations are observed in KMeans-expanded features, confirming their enhanced discriminative power in the models. The K_4 feature is particularly notable, displaying well-resolved peaks across concentrations, which effectively differentiates concentration levels and validates its high importance in machine learning models.

By comparison with other approaches, the present method for DA detection demonstrates superior performance (Table 1 ), as evidenced by its wider dynamic range and significantly lower LOD. Furthermore, the integration of cotton swab and smartphone with machine learning has rendered DA detection more portable and intelligent.

To validate the accuracy and feasibility of B, N-CDs for DA detection in real samples, plasma and urine samples were preprocessed following the protocol detailed in the experimental section and subsequently diluted one hundred-fold for analysis. DA was not detected in any of the processed real samples, including plasma and urine samples. Recovery experiments are conducted using DA-spiked plasma and urine samples at varying concentrations (Table 2 ). The results demonstrate recovery rates for DA ranging from 95.8 % to 99.0 % within the concentration range of 40–60 μg/L, with RSDs less than 7.09 %. For further validation, the same samples were analyzed using HPLC. A comparative analysis shows that the results obtained from the B, N-CDs method are in good agreement with those from the reference HPLC method. These findings confirm that B, N-CDs can be reliably employed for DA detection in complex real samples.

HRTEM analysis reveals that B, N-CDs exhibit spherical morphology with uniform dispersion. However, significant aggregation is observed upon the introduction of DA, as illustrated in Fig. 1A and C. This aggregation phenomenon is further confirmed by DLS measurement, which demonstrates an increase in particle size of B, N-CDs from 220 nm to 459 nm (Fig. S9, in Supplementary data). Zeta potential results of B, N-CDs provide additional insights, revealing a substantial decrease in surface potential from +23.01 mV to +9.35 mV upon DA addition (Fig. S10, in Supplementary data). Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of B, N-CDs + DA increases notably by 0.19 ns, rising from 2.87 ns to 3.06 ns compared to B, N-CDs (Fig. S11, in Supplementary data). These collective findings suggest that DA molecules cause the aggregation of B, N-CDs, leading to Aggregation-Induced Luminescence Enhancement (AIEE) [44,45]. The observed phenomena can be attributed to strong intermolecular interactions between B, N-CDs and DA, primarily mediated through hydrogen bonding and π-π stacking interactions [46,47]. These interactions effectively restrict the intramolecular vibrations and rotations of B, N-CDs, thereby significantly reducing the probability of non-radiative transition of B, N-CDs. Consequently, two distinct optical phenomena are observed: a blue shift in fluorescence peak and the emergence of AIEE characteristics in the B, N-CDs system.

In this study, a dual-mode spectroscopy platform utilizing B, N-CDs was successfully developed. The synergistic effects of ratio fluorescence and colorimetry were leveraged to achieve highly sensitive and specific detection of DA in complex biological matrices. B, N-CDs were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method using 3-APBA and Pro as precursors. B, N-CDs exhibited a pronounced dual response upon DA exposure. One was a decrease and an increase in fluorescence intensity at 420 nm and 465 nm, respectively, forming ratio fluorescence mode. The other one was a significant enhancement in absorbance was detected. Excellent linear relationships were demonstrated by both detection methods within the range of 2.5–500 μM, LOD determined to be 0.22 μM (ratio fluorometry) and 1.04 μM (colorimetry), respectively. Furthermore, RGB color analysis from smartphone was innovatively integrated with machine learning algorithm to develop an intelligent real-time detection system. It was capable of completing visual recognition and accurate quantification of DA. By combining the high sensitivity of the fluorescence method with the broad dynamic range of colorimetry, reliance on sophisticated instrumentation was effectively overcome. This platform is proposed as a novel, efficient, and cost-effective strategy for rapid field analysis in biomedical diagnostics, neurotransmitter monitoring, and other relevant fields.

Xiaoshuang Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lijun Wang: Supervision. Mengnan Wu: Supervision. Yihao Zheng: Supervision, Software. Ruirui Wang: Supervision. Tong Shao: Supervision. Suyuan Zeng: Data curation. Aifeng Li: Data curation. Rui Li: Supervision. Qiaoli Yue: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

This work was supported financially by China Scholarship Council program (202208370086), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022MB052), Graduate Education Quality Improvement Plan of Shandong Province (SDYJG21198) and research foundation of Liaocheng University (318050022 and 318012116).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
